paragraphs.text: Sky is one of Europe's largest content creation and distribution businesses, serving millions of customers across six countries. We operate both as a major content provider—through our commercial broadcasting and video-on-demand services—and as a leading communications provider in the fixed and mobile sectors.

We welcome the opportunity to contribute to the European Commission's consultation on the Digital Fairness Act. As a consumer-facing business, we are deeply committed to upholding the highest standards of consumer protection. Our ongoing efforts to innovate and enhance the customer experience go hand in hand with our responsibility to provide a safe, transparent, and respectful environment that supports users' rights and choices. Our business model is built around meeting consumer demand and preferences.

We ensure compliance with horizontal EU consumer protection legislation, as well as sector-specific frameworks such as the Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD) and the European Electronic Communications Code (EECC). These existing rules already cover many aspects of online consumer protection—including recommender systems, advertising, contracts, and communications. We therefore urge the Commission to avoid introducing additional measures which risk creating duplication, regulatory uncertainty, and instability. Instead, greater focus should be placed on the consistent and correct implementation of current rules, such as the right of withdrawal under Articles 9 and 16 of the Consumer Rights Directive.

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading designs
use of sensitive data
personalisation

paragraphs.text: We recognise the Commission's concerns around certain online practices—such as misleading designs, use of sensitive data, and risks to minors. While some practices may be misused in parts of the digital ecosystem, it is important not to generalise or label well-established, consumer-friendly, and already regulated features—such as recommendations, autoplay, and personalisation—as inherently problematic. As mentioned above, Sky operates in sectors where these practices are already subject to detailed regulation. Broad horizontal interventions could unintentionally disrupt responsible operators, even if they are not the intended targets.
Our views on the Commission's key areas of interest are elaborated in the sections below.

We welcome the European Commission's efforts to simplify the existing regulatory framework, fostering a more innovative and competitive European industry. Any eventual intervention in consumer legislation should be evidence-based, targeted, and focused on reducing compliance burdens for businesses—without compromising consumer protection standards. It should also take into account existing obligations and avoid unnecessary duplication.

While we do not see a need for additional regulation in this area, we believe the EU could benefit from streamlining and clarifying certain existing rules—through effective enforcement or, where appropriate, non-binding guidance. A relevant example is the right of withdrawal under Articles 9 and 16(m) of the Consumer Rights Directive (CRD, 2011/83/EU).

evidence_quotes.quote: waive their 14-day withdrawal right

paragraphs.text: Currently, the implementation of the right of withdrawal across Member States is fragmented and, in some cases, unclear – particularly regarding the waiver applicable to digital content providers. According to Article 16 of the CRD, suppliers of digital content may ask users to waive their 14-day withdrawal right, provided they have given prior consent to begin the performance of the contract.

evidence_quotes.quote: video-on-demand (VOD) subscriptions
contracts for these audiovisual subscription services should be treated as contracts for the supply of digital content

paragraphs.text: In the case of audio-visual media services (AVMS), in particular Pay-TV and video-on-demand (VOD) subscriptions, consumers purchase access to a specific catalogue of content, which they can begin consuming immediately. Without access to this content, the platform offers no material benefit. Browsing the user-interface or receiving recommendations is meaningless without the ability to watch content. Therefore, contracts for these audiovisual subscription services should be treated as contracts for the supply of digital content. Unfortunately, this interpretation has not been consistently applied across Europe, thus leading to fragmentation in the internal market.

evidence_quotes.quote: Imposing a mandatory 14-day withdrawal right for live and on-demand video services would undermine AVMS' business model.
This risk is exacerbated with regards to live sports subscriptions, where the entire commercial value for live premium content such as sports exists during the duration of the event.

paragraphs.text: Imposing a mandatory 14-day withdrawal right for live and on-demand video services would undermine AVMS' business model. It would allow consumers to access and consume high-value content upon sign-up, then cancel and request a refund, with little to no compensation for the creative investments made by the company. This creates a high risk of abuse and significant financial harm to providers, especially those offering premium films and TV series, where substantial value can be consumed in a short time. This risk is exacerbated with regards to live sports subscriptions, where the entire commercial value for live premium content such as sports exists during the duration of the event. As such, the value of these subscriptions lies not only in being able to access exclusive content from anywhere, but also in being able to access it at a specific time.

evidence_quotes.quote: if refunds were to be implemented on a pro-rata basis
calculate pro-rata refunds on a day-by-day basis for the first 14 days of their contract
the cost to business of implementing such measures from a technological 'build' perspective would be disproportionate to the consumer outcome
bespoke back-end solutions

paragraphs.text: When looking at this issue, it is also worth acknowledging how refunds would work in practice. Sky's perspective is there would be no workable solution in how to implement such a refund. For example, if refunds were to be implemented on a pro-rata basis, businesses would have to implement complicated, bespoke back-end solutions that would be able to calculate pro-rata refunds on a day-by-day basis for the first 14 days of their contract across the full suite of variable contracts a service offers. The cost to business of implementing such measures from a technological 'build' perspective would be disproportionate to the consumer outcome, and such a system would not reflect the investment in content rights that Sky broadcasts. The consequence of potentially having to meet such a measure would risk impacting the amount of funding we are able to invest in high end content and products our customers enjoy.

evidence_quotes.quote: waive their right of withdrawal once content consumption begins

paragraphs.text: In light of this, we would welcome legally binding clarification at EU level confirming: (1) that Pay-TV/SVOD content qualifies as digital content (for the purpose of the right of withdrawal); and (2) that Pay-TV/VOD providers are entitled to request users to waive their right of withdrawal once content consumption begins – thus extending the exception provided for in Article 16(m) to AVMS as well.

evidence_quotes.quote: a right to cancel at the end of the first month subject to paying for that whole one month (on a pro rata temporis basis) regardless of the formal duration of the contract.

paragraphs.text: We take note of the Commission's proposal to replace the 14-days right of withdrawal with a right to cancel at the end of the first month subject to paying for that whole one month (on a pro rata temporis basis) regardless of the formal duration of the contract. We stand ready to exchange with the Commission on existing practices, but for now key concerns remain in terms of both exact regulatory requirements and the technical and commercial feasibility of such a solution, which would differ for different business models. We would thus welcome further clarification from the Commission on the exact scope and details of such a proposal.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalisation practices allow users to experience a curated offer that is tailored to their preferences and needs.

paragraphs.text: As a TV platform provider, our priority is to ensure that viewers enjoy their experience and choose to stay with us because they value what we offer. It is therefore in our interest to maintain a transparent and user-friendly interface. At the same time, our viewers choose Sky for the curated experience we provide—an experience shaped by our editorial decisions and responsibilities, which are already governed by audiovisual legislation. Such personalisation practices allow users to experience a curated offer that is tailored to their preferences and needs.

These two elements—user choice and editorial responsibility—are not in conflict; rather, they complement each other. To deliver the best possible experience, it is essential that we retain flexibility and autonomy in the design and configuration of our user interfaces.

evidence_quotes.quote: These tools should not be deemed ‘problematic’ or ‘manipulative’ by default.

paragraphs.text: With this in mind, we encourage the Commission to assess interface features such as **autoplay** and **recommender systems** in a nuanced way. These tools should not be deemed ‘problematic’ or ‘manipulative’ by default. As a responsible media company, our use of these features does not promote addictive behaviours, but is used to reduce the time in which it takes users to discover the content they know and love.

evidence_quotes.quote: Our recommendations are based on user preferences and viewing history (e.g. recently watched content)

paragraphs.text: Autoplay on our platform is limited to advancing to the next episode in a series, and users can easily disable this feature via their account settings if they so wish. Our recommendations are based on user preferences and viewing history (e.g. recently watched content), combined with editorial choices informed by context such as time, location, and relevant events (e.g. sports competitions or new releases). Importantly, these recommendations do not rely on sensitive personal data nor exploit user vulnerabilities. Moreover, our content offering is curated and regulated under the AVMSD, with a strong emphasis on promoting cultural diversity and pluralism.

evidence_quotes.quote: caution against the introduction of broad, horizontal interventions that indiscriminately target interface management practices.

paragraphs.text: Limiting - even inadvertently - the legitimate use of these tools would risk diminishing the overall user choice and experience by making content discovery less intuitive and reducing convenience for viewers. These tools help users navigate large content libraries, access relevant programming, and enjoy seamless viewing. We thus caution against the introduction of broad, horizontal interventions that indiscriminately target interface management practices. Such measures risk undermining the balance between user empowerment and editorial responsibility that is central to delivering a positive media experience for audiences.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalisation features switched off by default.

paragraphs.text: As a family brand in millions of homes in Europe, the safety of our customers and their families is of fundamental importance to us. We have a high-quality, dedicated in-house compliance team in place that reviews our content based on both European and national rules and respects local sensibilities. We are very careful in making sure children can enjoy a safe experience without any risk of being exposed to inappropriate content. The ‘kids section’ on our interface provides that safe space with minors—appropriate content recommendations and personalisation features switched off by default. Age ratings also allow us to implement further parental controls such as pin access.

evidence_quotes.quote: 18+ rated websites are automatically blocked when you're connected to the Sky Mobile network.

paragraphs.text: Our commitment to child protection online is not limited to our TV products. For instance, 18+ rated websites are automatically blocked when you're connected to the Sky Mobile network. Each SIM has different settings, enabling users to turn off parental controls for themselves (after having verified that the person removing them is over 18) and leave it on for members of their family, including kids. Sky Wifi customers also can choose to activate parental controls, hence blocking access to websites deemed inappropriate for kids.

All of the above goes to show that we already have an extensive set of protection measures in place when it comes to minors' activity online. This is also in response to the high number of regulatory requirements already in place for both audiovisual services (AVMSD, Art. 28) and electronic communications services, including GDPR. There is little to no evidence of harm to children on regulated audiovisual services. Any intervention in this space should thus be based on concrete evidence and targeted to those business models/sectors that are found to be lacking sufficient protection measures.

evidence_quotes.quote: use—or non-use—of personal and sensitive data for targeting purposes.

paragraphs.text: As with other areas discussed above, advertising in the audiovisual sector is already subject to extensive regulation. In addition to complying with the quantitative and qualitative requirements set out in the AVMSD, we also adhere to the GDPR when it comes to the use—or non-use—of personal and sensitive data for targeting purposes. This is further complemented by national-level rules, such as local implementations of the ePrivacy directive and the Irish Data Protection Commission's guidance on child-oriented data processing.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalised advertising that target minors nor exploit emotional or financial vulnerabilities

paragraphs.text: In our TV products, we do not engage in personalised advertising that target minors nor exploit emotional or financial vulnerabilities, and we apply robust safeguards – such as household-level targeting and the use of a walled garden environment with no spillover to online behavioural advertising – to ensure our targeted TV advertising remains appropriate and limited in intrusiveness.

evidence_quotes.quote: We provide prominent and detailed information about our personalisation practices, including how users can opt out.

paragraphs.text: We provide prominent and detailed information about our personalisation practices, including how users can opt out. Sky's Privacy Notice (available in every market in which we operate) includes a dedicated section that clearly and repeatedly explains how users can opt out of targeted advertising and profiling. In 2023, we further enhanced our transparency efforts by

evidence_quotes.quote: tailored advertising practices.

paragraphs.text: launching the Sky Privacy Centre – a resource where users can access FAQs and key facts about how we handle personal data. Thanks to these practices, we do not typically receive customer questions or complaints about our tailored advertising practices.

evidence_quotes.quote: When implemented within the safeguards of existing legislation, personalisation can be a valuable tool to tailor the user experience to individual preferences and needs.

paragraphs.text: When implemented within the safeguards of existing legislation, personalisation can be a valuable tool to tailor the user experience to individual preferences and needs. Blanket characterisations of personalisation as inherently 'problematic'—and proposals to ban such practices by default—would be both disproportionate and unnecessary. It would also be damaging to our business model and the financial sustainability of the sector.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalisation

paragraphs.text: We acknowledge and support the Commission's ongoing simplification measures on digital regulation, specifically in the framework of the upcoming Digital Omnibus package. We understand this would also address elements of the GDPR and ePrivacy Directive. We would welcome clarification on how this strand of work would interact with the discussions around personalisation (and not only) within the Digital Fairness Act.

evidence_quotes.quote: our subscription models are tailored to reflect the diverse preferences and needs of our customers.

paragraphs.text: As a provider of audiovisual and telecommunications services, our contract and subscription practices are governed by a comprehensive set of existing legislation—both horizontal (such as the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive, Unfair Contract Terms Directive, and Consumer Rights Directive) and sector-specific (notably the European Electronic Communications Code). Given the wide range of products and services we offer, our subscription models are tailored to reflect the diverse preferences and needs of our customers.

evidence_quotes.quote: This diversity means that a one-size-fits-all approach to contracts and subscriptions is neither practical nor desirable.

paragraphs.text: This diversity means that a one-size-fits-all approach to contracts and subscriptions is neither practical nor desirable. Businesses like ours must retain a degree of flexibility in how we design and offer our services, in order to continue meeting consumer expectations effectively and responsibly.

Sky welcomes the European Commission's commitment to ensuring fairness and transparency for consumers in the digital environment. As a consumer-facing business operating in highly regulated sectors, we believe that any future intervention should not duplicate existing frameworks. The Audiovisual Media Services Directive, the Electronic Communications Code,

evidence_quotes.quote: personalisation

paragraphs.text: and the GDPR already provide robust safeguards for consumers, including minors, and regulate key practices such as advertising, personalisation, and user interface design.

We thus encourage the Commission to consider carefully whether any intervention is needed in this area. If deemed necessary, a targeted, evidence-based approach should be taken – one that recognises the diversity of business models and avoids one-size-fits-all solutions. Preserving flexibility for responsible operators is essential to maintaining innovation, consumer choice, editorial integrity and financial sustainability.

We remain committed to constructive dialogue and stand ready to support the Commission in shaping a regulatory environment that protects users while enabling Europe's digital and creative industries to thrive.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
